skill.name: entra-agent-id
skill.description: Guides Microsoft Entra Agent Identity setup for blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, per-agent identities, OAuth token exchange, OBO flows, and the AgentID sidecar.
skill.summary: Implement Microsoft Entra Agent Identity provisioning and runtime token exchange.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, credential, and obfuscation patterns, but review found they are in Markdown guidance and sample code for Microsoft Graph and Entra token flows. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill remains medium risk because it guides privileged identity provisioning, token exchange, credential handling, and permission grants.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Microsoft Graph Identity Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill provides examples for creating Agent Identity Blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, Agent Identities, app role assignments, and delegated permission grants. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but misuse can create identities or grant Microsoft Graph permissions with broad tenant impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly call Microsoft Graph endpoints that create resources or grant permissions. Context shows administrative setup guidance, not malicious behavior, so the risk is operational rather than hostile.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Examples Require Careful Use
medium_findings.description: The skill shows Azure credential environment variables, client secret creation, and sidecar secret configuration. These patterns are expected for Entra setup, but users could leak secrets if copied into logs, code, or insecure deployment manifests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly discuss real client secrets and environment-based credentials. The same files recommend production alternatives, which lowers confidence that this is unsafe by design.

medium_findings.title: Unauthenticated Sidecar Endpoints Must Stay Local
medium_findings.description: The AgentID sidecar documentation includes unauthenticated token endpoints for local companion-container use. The deployment guidance warns not to expose the SDK publicly, but a user who publishes it through ingress or a load balancer could expose token acquisition functionality.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint table explicitly marks unauthenticated endpoints, and the deployment file documents the required containment. The risk depends on deployment choices, so this is a medium-confidence operational concern.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Markdown code fences or inline command references for installing modules, Azure CLI lookup, PowerShell Graph requests, and troubleshooting. They are not hidden executable skill logic and do not run automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are visible Markdown examples and prose. There is no script file, hook, or automatic execution path in the skill structure.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive-File and Weak-Crypto Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM alert appears to match the term SAML in authentication documentation, and weak-crypto alerts appear tied to OAuth terminology such as client secrets or RFC identifiers. These are not evidence of local secret theft or unsafe cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is identity documentation and JWT validation guidance. I found no evidence of access to Windows SAM files or custom weak cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Microsoft Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded network locations point to Microsoft Learn, Microsoft Graph, login.microsoftonline.com, GitHub documentation, localhost, or container images. These endpoints match the stated Entra Agent Identity workflow and do not indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are first-party Microsoft identity endpoints or documentation links. No suspicious third-party collection endpoint was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Administrative Graph Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Sample code performs Microsoft Graph writes that can create identities and grant application or delegated permissions. Users should run these only in approved tenants with least-privilege roles and change control.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are explicit Graph write operations in setup examples. They are not malicious, but they are sensitive administrative actions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token Exchange and Secret Material in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The runtime examples pass client secrets, client assertions, parent tokens, and user tokens through OAuth flows. This is core functionality, but copied implementations need secret storage, logging controls, and tenant validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly handle sensitive token material. The behavior is expected for an authentication skill, so the finding is a caution rather than a block.

content.value_statement: Agent identity setup requires precise Graph objects, credentials, and token exchange rules. This skill gives structured guidance for Microsoft Entra Agent ID provisioning, OBO flows, and sidecar deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains the Blueprint, BlueprintPrincipal, and Agent Identity object model.
Provides Microsoft Graph workflows for creating blueprints and per-agent identities.
Guides autonomous and OBO fmi_path token exchange flows.
Documents permission grants for app-only and delegated Microsoft Graph access.
Covers the Microsoft Entra SDK for AgentID sidecar configuration and deployment.
Lists common Agent ID errors and practical troubleshooting steps.
limitations: Does not replace Microsoft Graph permissions, tenant roles, or administrator approval.
Uses sample code that must be adapted and reviewed before production use.
Requires current Microsoft documentation checks because Agent ID APIs may change.
Does not manage standard app registration, Azure RBAC, or Foundry agent authoring.

use_cases.title: Provision Per-Agent Identities
use_cases.description: Create a Blueprint, BlueprintPrincipal, and separate Agent Identities for auditable agent instances.

use_cases.title: Design Agent Token Exchange
use_cases.description: Implement autonomous or on-behalf-of Graph access with fmi_path and per-agent permission boundaries.

use_cases.title: Deploy AgentID Sidecar Authentication
use_cases.description: Configure the Microsoft Entra SDK for AgentID sidecar for polyglot agents in Docker or Kubernetes.

prompt_templates.title: Explain The Object Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Agent Identity Blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, and Agent Identities relate to each other. Include when each object is created.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A New Agent Identity Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Microsoft Entra Agent ID setup for three agent instances. Include required Graph permissions, sponsors, and safe credential choices.

prompt_templates.title: Debug A Token Exchange Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: I am getting AADSTS700211 during fmi_path token exchange. Walk me through tenant, FIC, audience, scope, and Agent Identity checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review A Production Sidecar Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my AgentID sidecar deployment design for production. Focus on localhost binding, workload identity, secret handling, and permission grants.

output_examples.input: Plan a new Entra Agent ID deployment for a customer support agent.
output: A step-by-step provisioning plan for the Blueprint, BlueprintPrincipal, and agent instances.
A list of required Graph permissions and consent steps.
Credential guidance that prefers workload identity for production.

output_examples.input: Troubleshoot why my OBO exchange returns AADSTS50013.
output: A short diagnosis that checks the user token audience first.
Required Blueprint API configuration items for access_as_user.
Next checks for granted delegated scopes and token exchange parameters.

output_examples.input: Assess my sidecar deployment before release.
output: A security checklist for local-only sidecar exposure.
Recommendations for workload identity and secret separation.
Operational checks for health probes, logs, and permission propagation delays.

best_practices: Verify Microsoft Graph endpoints and request shapes against current Microsoft Learn documentation before running commands.
Use workload identity or federated identity credentials for production instead of client secrets.
Grant permissions per Agent Identity and keep the AgentID sidecar reachable only from trusted local workloads.
anti_patterns: Do not expose unauthenticated sidecar endpoints through public ingress, load balancers, or shared networks.
Do not grant broad Graph scopes to every Agent Identity without a per-agent access review.
Do not paste generated client secrets into source control, chat logs, or container manifests.

faq.question: Is this skill for normal Entra app registration?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on Entra Agent Identity Blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, Agent Identities, and related token flows.

faq.question: Can it create resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and sample requests. A user or automation must run commands with appropriate tenant permissions.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill handles privileged identity, token, secret, and permission workflows. The reviewed content appears legitimate but requires careful use.

faq.question: Does it support OBO authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Blueprint API setup, delegated grants, user token audience requirements, and the OBO token exchange path.

faq.question: Can it help with non-.NET agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers the Microsoft Entra SDK for AgentID sidecar for Python, Node, Go, Java, and other runtimes.

faq.question: What should I verify before production use?
faq.answer: Verify current Microsoft documentation, least-privilege Graph grants, secure credential storage, and local-only sidecar exposure.
